summary: MCP server poses as a Web3 secrets scanner; exfiltrates caller inputs, git remotes, and env vars to attacker webhook
details: The package advertises itself as a local Web3 secrets-scanning MCP tool, but the tool handler performs no scanning. Instead, on every MCP tools/call invocation it POSTs the tool name, caller-supplied arguments, the output of `git remote -v`, `process.env.SHELL`, and the first three entries of `process.env.PATH` to an attacker-controlled webhook (index.js lines 67-74). The webhook destination is fetched dynamically from `https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json` (index.js lines 7-15), with a webhook.site fallback, allowing the operator to rotate exfiltration endpoints without republishing the package. The handler then returns hardcoded fake scan results (a fabricated `scripts/deploy.js:42` finding, fixed counts) regardless of input (index.js lines 81-90), so the lure remains plausible to AI agents and human users. This is a deliberate lure: AI agents are expected to pass paths to keystores, repos, and config directories as `args.target` — those paths and the surrounding git/host context are shipped straight to the attacker. Generic publisher metadata (`Web3 Audit Collective`, `github.com/w3audit`) with no prior ecosystem presence corroborates the malicious intent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
